I was working on writing something to say to you guys, but I'm so nervous I figured I'd forget it anyway, so I just thought I'd get up here and start talking and hopefully it'll lead somewhere. I really, when David was talking yesterday about making sacrifices and Marcello was talking about that too, going to Baltimore and working for them, it really had a nerve with me because that's exactly what I did to jump start my career. When I was, it was in June of 2009 that I first got the email from the total package and they were looking for somebody to work in the house with them. At the time I was living in Detroit, I'm still living in Detroit, but at the time I had, you know, I have three kids, married, had a full-time career, and when I got this it was, I had been working for four years. I got with 808 AI in 2005, started with, can you write a letter like this? It's the first time I heard about copywriting, I had no idea the career even existed, but it really had a nerve with me, so I started studying it. I did what you guys, a lot of you guys are doing, I worked nights, I worked weekends studying this stuff, I read sales letters that worked, I got on the mailing list, I went through the courses, but I really didn't get anywhere with it. The reason being, I'm extreme introvert, at least I was, I've come out a lot more now, but I was an extreme introvert at the time, and I was really attracted to copywriting because I didn't have to deal with people, at least so I thought. So you know, it's, it's, yeah, it's, you know, it's this career where you work from home and you don't have to, you know, at the time it's like, oh, you don't have to talk to people, you just, you know, but obviously that's a bunch of bull because you do have to talk to people and you do have to get to know people. So, but anyways, when this, when this email came, fortunately I had a spouse that was very much on board with what I was doing and believed in the dream with me, and she was very supportive, and I actually ended up, I applied at the 11th hour, I know Wendy, I don't know if Wendy remembers this, but the contest, it was literally three or four o'clock on a Friday when the contest was about to end, and I had seen these emails coming for probably a week, and I just kept deleting them because it's like, oh, this would be awesome, yeah, it would really jumpstart my career. But I was, I was like, I deleted them because I thought, you know, I had that whole thing going on in my head where I'm not good enough for this, I've never done this, I've read the books, but I don't have any experience, I've never written a single letter. Well, at the time I had done a couple of specs, but that was it. So, I just deleted them, and then finally, I guess it just kind of clicked on my head, yeah. Oh, I'm sorry. So, finally it clicked on my head, and I'm like, you know, what the heck do I have to lose? I'm sitting here at this job that I hate, I hate coming here every day, it sucks, you know, I'm working on this stuff at night, what the heck, what have I got to lose? And I sent it in, and actually I contacted Wendy and she was nice enough to let me, she's like, okay, I'll give you a couple days, no problem, and yeah. So, I wrote the sales letter over the weekend, and I sent it in, and lo and behold, I ended up getting flown out, my wife and I, they flew us out to North Carolina and offered me the position, and it was like, you know, it was like wonder struck, but the thing is, we couldn't sell our house in Detroit, this was in 2009, 2000, you know, the housing market just had collapsed, Detroit was one of the hardest hit, we lost I think 40% of our equity in our house, so I actually, I moved to North Carolina and left my family behind, and actually worked in North Carolina for nine months, and my kids and my wife were still back home, so, but like I said, fortunately my wife was incredibly supportive, she knew what I, you know, she knew the dream, she understood it, so we made it work, and after nine months, Wendy and Clayton came to me and said, we don't want to be responsible for your divorce, so just go home, so, I ended up working remotely, but that, I think that speaks to the sacrifice, you know, you do have to make sacrifices sometimes, you might get lucky, you might be in your back door, some of you, I've talked to some of you who live in Baltimore, that's awesome, if you can get in Baltimore, you're obviously already there, but sometimes you gotta make the sacrifice, and to me it was well worth it, I knew what working with Clayton would do to my career, I knew it would, that would be the wow factor, you know, so, fortunately I got lucky enough, again, of course I had Clayton on my resume at this time when Jeff Walker came along and I ended up working with Jeff too, which has been amazing, because, you know, when they talk about learning another skill, it's, I can't tell you how important that is as a copywriter, because, in fact, I've had, I've probably talked to half of you already, I've come up to me and asked me questions about product launches instead of copywriting, because it's so critical, especially right now. One thing I wanted to, one thing I really wanted to say was, you know, we talked a lot, Clayton's talked a lot about it, we always talk about these huge financial projects, the huge health projects, all these big companies, if you're just starting out, please, please, please don't overlook local clients, smaller companies, all these other clients, like if you're just starting out, you know, with the copywriting and the product launch formula skills I have, I can walk into a smaller client or somebody who's just starting out, their experts are a dime a dozen, there's a lot of great experts out there that have a lot of great skills, but they suck at marketing themselves. So, I could probably go to Amazon tomorrow and I could look up 40 experts and send them a letter and I would have a contract with them tomorrow. And these guys, when you walk in there, you're a marketing god, they look at you like, oh my god, because they don't know this stuff. So, you know, you can offer them, there's a million ways to do it to line up a contract with these guys, but they're just sitting out there and you think, you know, they're on Amazon and they got their book out there, but, you know, they might be getting two sales a month, they might not be getting no sales, but their content is awesome. So, you know, that's a great way to start, keep that in mind. You can find these people, just send them a letter and talk to them. So, let's see. If I was just starting out today, that's what I would do immediately. I hit my notes, but I forgot my notes. Oh, another thing, I'm going to give some tips since I'm, people are talking about, who was it that said something about sleeping with the bowl and then the bowl falls? You know, it kind of wakes you up and you're in this state, David. Well, they, you know, that's all about the theta brain waves and the alpha brain waves and all this stuff. Well, the tip is if I actually have audios, you can buy audios that put you in, put your mind in that state. And if I'm having trouble creatively, I just put on the headphones and I listened for a few minutes and, you know, within five minutes, I'm sitting or typing while I'm listening to it because it actually puts your mind in that state and that, in that brainwave state. I got mine from Subconscious Training Corporation, I believe is the company. So, well worth it. Questions. Anybody have questions? Because... So, how did you decide in that moment to take that leap of faith and say yes to Clayton, to turn your life upside down like that? I think it was because I was miserable. Yeah. So, the pain of not doing anything. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, at first it's like you're excited about a new career possibility and you want to do it and, but, you know, for somebody like me, especially who was afraid to take the leap because I was such, being such an introvert and so afraid of getting in front of people, that stopped me. But I think the fact that I was in corporate America, I hated corporate America, I'm not a corporate guy. They just, not me, I got a, you know, I'm a free bird. So, I think we probably all are. Yeah. So, I think it's the pain of finally getting to the point where I just couldn't take it anymore and I decided to take the chance and take the risk. Yeah. How are you reaching out to new clients now with the skill sets you have now? Better not be. You know what? To be completely honest with you, I haven't looked for a client in probably four years. I haven't, once picked up the phone, I haven't had to look for one. I haven't searched for one. What's that? Can you repeat the question to talk to me so we can hear you? When you answer. Oh, I'm sorry. The question was, how am I getting in contacting new clients right now? And my answer was, I haven't done it in four years. I'm turning clients away even right now. So, clearly you're making the faith pay off. Clearly. Yeah. Data. What did you learn working directly with Clayton that he wouldn't have learned reading his stuff or studying his stuff? He's got to be a good thing. You know, I honestly, actually I ended up working far more with Wendy than I did with Clayton. At the time Clayton was working out of his house and we saw him in the office once every couple of weeks, I guess, maybe. But it was mostly working with Wendy on the day to day and Wendy is sharp as a tack. I learned more about marketing and stuff from her from working with her than she's not even in the room. I learned more from her as far as that part of the business, as far as the scheduling and how to, you know, the first email you send out is like, you know, big headline. And then the second email you send out is just a shorter, you know, that kind of stuff. I learned more about that from her. Working with Clayton, I think it was just the value of the crits and seeing the red copy come back and, you know, being able to deal with that. What are your tips for other introverts in this room? I mean, you've been very successful as an introvert marketing yourself and learning how to do that. I think that's really valuable for people to hear. Yeah. I think, how do you market yourself as an introvert? I think, you know, some of the people have asked me what I would do today if I was starting out. And other than the fact that I would go to Amazon and call all these people and say, hey, I can make you, you know, because, you know, all kinds of money, just give me whatever, you know, whatever percentage. I would just get on the mailing list and I would just, you can do all this from email and phone. You don't have to get in front of people. You can get on all their email list, see all the letters they're sending out, you know, get to know their company, get to know their products, pick a product you like, and then write an email, write, not an email, but write a, write something for it, write a couple pages, write a spec and get it to them. Find out who the contact is and get it to them. And you can get jobs without even seeing each other. And you really can. I didn't, I didn't know all this at the time, but I do now. And that's how I would do it now. Absolutely. Yeah. How did you, your product launch formula part of your career? Did you know that before you came in? Did you learn it when you started? Didn't know it at all. After I, after I left working for Weiss initially and working for Clayton initially, I went out on my own for a few months and then I kind of, he had the make peace business system, which basically tells you how to become a partner and, you know, an actual partner with your client. And I also picked up on the value of having something to separate you from other copywriters, the extra skill. And when Jeff came along, it's funny because I ended up with Jeff the exact same way. Ended up with Clayton. I got an email from Jeff saying, we're looking for a new team member. And, you know, I thought about her awhile and I just jumped on the opportunity and I took, it actually cost me some time and some money to do that because I worked, I made a lot less working for Jeff full time than I would have as a freelance copywriter. But the payoff now in my career, I can probably never quantitate what it's going to do to my career to no product launch formula. So I did it. I, you know, I took the gamble and, and got with him and did that. So sacrifices, it's about, you got to make some sacrifices. I didn't know, I hadn't remembered what role he played around there in terms of creating a new team. But, you know, when she and I first met, it was because she was creating my copy. Oh, at Phillips. She was a marketing director at Phillips and she called me up nervous as a cat talking to Clayton and she goes, well, sir, my job is to convey to you the comments on your copy on your latest draft that the team had. And now if you don't like any of these, Chris, you don't have to do anything with them. It's totally up to you. It was like, please don't hit me. You know, that was our very first conversation. And then she, she went through my copy and she found some really good stuff and then it really helped it. So, yeah. Yeah. So, you know, something else I want to say was lots of times you learn more from the mistakes. I'd say I always learn more from my mistakes than, than my, you know, when the good stuff goes because you tend to, you tend to reflect on your mistakes and you tend to impact you a heck of a lot more. The biggest mistake I ever made was trying to be something I wasn't to a client. I had this big client and I had done work for them and everything was going great. I had a retainer with them and they gave me a really big project with a really unreasonable timeframe. And at the time I was still relatively newbie, a newbie and I guess I really didn't have the full confidence in myself that I now knew what I was doing yet as far as understanding that stuff and I figured they knew more than me because they were more experienced. So, I accepted it. And long story short, I ended up losing the client. I ended up destroying my own confidence in the process because I had, you know, after I accepted the project and wasn't able to complete it, I, I can't, I don't know how many sleepless nights I had. Just lying awake, literally cold sweats. It was horrible. It was the worst part, you know, worst thing in my life. But the value that I learned from it was, you know, dude, if you can't take the assignment, if you can't do it, if you think it's unreasonable, tell them that. Because later I realized that, later on I learned that what they were asking me to do couldn't have been done by a professional. I mean Clayton couldn't have done it because the timeframe was so incredibly unreasonable. It was, it was ridiculous. But I didn't know that at the time. I didn't trust myself. So, don't be something or not. Be real. And something I learned from Jeff Walker, something that really stuck with me, all his videos, all his, you know, when he's on stage, all this stuff, he tells, he tells his origin story from where, where he came from constantly over and over and over again. And I got to the point where I could, I had heard this thing so many times I could recite the sucker myself. I thought I was Jeff Walker. So, and it's like, oh my God. But you see the incredible effect it has on people. This is what draws people to Jeff Walker. He tells his origin story and people realize that he is a real person. He came from the same place, they've been in probably. He was broke and that didn't have a job. So, you know, the whole thing that I got from that was just be real. People don't want to work with a corporation. They want to work with a person and they don't want to work with somebody who's fake. They want to work with somebody who's real, who has personality, who just wants to do a really good job with them, for them. So, just be yourself. If it loses you to a client, my wife actually said this, if you burn a bridge with somebody, it's a bridge that was probably never meant to be there in the first place. So, if you burn a bridge, move on. If it was probably not a client, you should have had it in the first place. So, just be yourself.
